ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wafer food product class; no specific entry for cream-filled rolled wafer stick exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers wafers as a biscuit/cracker subcategory. No specific cream-filled rolled wafer stick code exists; closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cream-filled wafer stick snack product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Apollo Wafer Stick is one small pack of approximately 25 g, containing 4–5 individual rolled wafer sticks.

nutrient_source.title: Apollo Wafer Stick — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Apollo Wafer Stick (any flavour), cr_id F11080002. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Wafer biscuits, cream-filled — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Apollo Wafer Stick specifically. GI of 76 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cream-filled wafer biscuits, which are compositionally analogous (refined wheat flour base, cream filling, negligible fibre, high sugar). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (76) × carbohydrate per serving (17.5 g per 25 g serve) / 100 = 13.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (29.09 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, independently elevating blood pressure risk; palm oil-derived saturated fat further contributes to vascular stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Apollo Wafer Sticks due to the combination of very high sugar (29.09 g/100 g), zero fibre, and likely high saturated fat from palm oil, all of which promote insulin-driven sodium retention and adverse cardiovascular outcomes per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Total carbohydrate of 70 g/100 g with sugar at 29.09 g/100 g and zero dietary fibre produces a high glycemic index (~76) and rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation with no fibre buffer.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Apollo Wafer Sticks entirely; the extremely high sugar (29.09 g/100 g), high total carbohydrate (70 g/100 g), and complete absence of dietary fibre result in rapid and significant blood glucose spikes that are incompatible with glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (21.36 g/100 g) predominantly from palm oil (estimated saturated fat ~11 g/100 g) combined with very high sugar and ultra-processed formulation raises LDL cholesterol and promotes atherogenic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Apollo Wafer Sticks due to high saturated fat from palm oil (~11 g/100 g estimated), very high sugar (29.09 g/100 g), and ultra-processed composition, all of which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and increased atherosclerotic cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA 2019 dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apollo Wafer Stick (Any Flavour) is a popular ultra-processed commercial snack widely sold in Singapore and Southeast Asia. It consists of a thin, crispy rolled wafer tube filled with a flavoured cream centre, available in variants such as chocolate, vanilla, strawberry, and pandan. The wafer shell is baked from refined wheat flour and the cream filling is made with vegetable fat (predominantly palm oil) and sugar, giving the product its characteristic light crunch and sweet, rich flavour. Per 100 g, the product provides 495 kcal, 5.45 g protein, 21.36 g fat (predominantly saturated from palm oil, estimated ~11 g/100 g), and 70 g carbohydrate of which 29.09 g is sugar, with zero dietary fibre and 62 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 76 (high), making it a high-glycaemic food that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. Clinically, this product is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its very high sugar content, high saturated fat, zero fibre, and ultra-processed formulation. It is best regarded as an occasional treat and should be consumed in strict moderation. Nutrient data sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080002, confidence 0.95).
common_user_queries: How many calories in Apollo wafer stick?
Can diabetics eat Apollo wafer stick?
Is Apollo wafer stick suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of Apollo wafer stick?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080002) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match to an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat estimated from palm oil composition typical of this product class; GI estimated from analogous cream-filled wafer biscuit entry in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is a refined wheat-based ultra-processed product with no fibre-containing ingredients. calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source. saturated_fat_g (~11 g/100 g) is estimated from palm oil composition typical of Apollo Wafer Stick formulation; the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields beyond calcium and iron are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this commercial wafer stick product are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cream-filled wafer biscuits); no direct GI measurement for Apollo Wafer Stick brand product was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving (25 g) = 76 × 17.5 / 100 = 13.3. The input health_suitability field referenced trans fat at 0.2 g/100 g; this value is not confirmed by the HPB source and has not been carried forward. Trans fat is set to -1 pending label verification.
